questions.specification: The time $T$ (in minutes) taken by a commuter to travel from home to work is normally distributed with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$. It is known that $P(T<45)=0.2266$ and $P(T>60)=0.2266$. The commuter must arrive by 08:00 to be on time, leaving home at 07:00. If the commute takes more than 65 minutes, the commuter takes a taxi, which costs extra.

parts.content: Find the values of $\mu$ and $\sigma$.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify probability regions: $P(T<60)=1-0.2266=0.7734$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Set up standardized normal equations: $\frac{45-\mu}{\sigma}=\Phi^{-1}(0.2266)\approx -0.75$ and $\frac{60-\mu}{\sigma}=\Phi^{-1}(0.7734)\approx 0.75$ <marks>A1</marks> <marks>A1</marks>

- Attempt to solve the system of linear equations: $45-\mu=-0.75\sigma$ and $60-\mu=0.75\sigma$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the values: $\mu=52.5, \sigma=10$ <marks>A1</marks> <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

steps.title: Analyze probability symmetry
steps.content:
The problem gives us two probabilities: the probability that the time is less than 45 minutes is $0.2266$, and the probability that it is greater than 60 minutes is also $0.2266$. 

Since these tail probabilities are equal, the normal distribution curve must be symmetric relative to these two values. This implies that the mean $\mu$ is exactly in the middle of $45$ and $60$.

steps.title: Calculate the mean
steps.content:
We can find the mean $\mu$ by calculating the midpoint of the two x-values:

$$\mu = \frac{45 + 60}{2} = 52.5$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate (45 + 60) / 2

ti84.instructions: Calculate (45 + 60) / 2

steps.title: Find the z-score
steps.content:
Now we need to find the standard deviation $\sigma$. We use the standard normal distribution to find the $z$-score corresponding to the lower tail area of $0.2266$.

Using the inverse normal function on your calculator:
$$\Phi^{-1}(0.2266) \approx -0.75$$

gdc.instructions: Go to Statistics > Distributions > Normal > Inverse Normal. Set Area: 0.2266, μ: 0, σ: 1.

ti84.instructions: Press 2nd > VARS (DISTR) > 3:invNorm. Enter area: 0.2266, μ: 0, σ: 1.

steps.title: Solve for standard deviation
steps.content:
We use the standardization formula $$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$ with our known values:
- $x = 45$ (the boundary for the lower tail)
- $\mu = 52.5$
- $z = -0.75$

Substitute these into the equation:

$$-0.75 = \frac{45 - 52.5}{\sigma}$$

steps.title: Calculate final value
steps.content:
Rearrange the equation to solve for $\sigma$:

$$\begin{aligned} -0.75\sigma &= 45 - 52.5 \\ -0.75\sigma &= -7.5 \\ \sigma &= \frac{-7.5}{-0.75} \\ \sigma &= 10 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the answers
steps.content:
The mean and standard deviation are:

$$\mu = 52.5, \quad \sigma = 10$$

steps.title: Identify probability regions
steps.content:
To find the mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, we first need to express the given information as cumulative probabilities (probabilities to the left of a value, $P(T \lt k)$), because the standard normal tables and calculator inverse functions work with the area to the left.

The first probability is already in the correct form:
$$P(T \lt 45) = 0.2266$$

The second probability is given as an area to the right. We convert this to the area to the left by subtracting from 1:

$$\begin{aligned} P(T \lt 60) &= 1 - P(T \gt 60) \\ &= 1 - 0.2266 \\ &= 0.7734 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the z-scores
steps.content:
This problem involves finding unknown parameters using the inverse normal distribution, which is covered in **Topic 4: Statistics & Probability (SL 4.12)**.

We use the inverse normal function on our calculator to find the standardized $z$-scores corresponding to these cumulative probabilities.

For $P(Z < z_1) = 0.2266$:
$$z_1 = -0.75$$

For $P(Z < z_2) = 0.7734$:
$$z_2 = 0.75$$

Notice that because the tail probabilities ($0.2266$) are identical, the $z$-scores are symmetric around 0.

gdc.instructions: Go to Statistics > Distributions > Normal > Inverse Normal. Set Tail: Left, Area: 0.2266, μ: 0, σ: 1.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR) > 3:invNorm. Enter area = 0.2266, μ = 0, σ = 1.

calculator.description: Find the z-scores using the Inverse Normal function

steps.title: Set up simultaneous equations
steps.content:
Using the standardization formula $$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$, we can set up two linear equations with our values ($x_1=45, z_1=-0.75$ and $x_2=60, z_2=0.75$).

Equation 1:
$$\frac{45 - \mu}{\sigma} = -0.75 \implies 45 - \mu = -0.75\sigma$$

Equation 2:
$$\frac{60 - \mu}{\sigma} = 0.75 \implies 60 - \mu = 0.75\sigma$$

steps.title: Solve the system
steps.content:
We now solve this system of linear equations. A simple method is to eliminate $\mu$ by subtracting Equation 1 from Equation 2.

$$
\begin{aligned} (60 - \mu) - (45 - \mu) &= 0.75\sigma - (-0.75\sigma) \\ 15 &= 1.5\sigma \\ \sigma &= 10 \end{aligned}
$$

Now, substitute $\sigma = 10$ back into Equation 2 to find $\mu$:

$$
\begin{aligned} 60 - \mu &= 0.75(10) \\ 60 - \mu &= 7.5 \\ \mu &= 60 - 7.5 \\ \mu &= 52.5 \end{aligned}
$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate 15 / 1.5

steps.content:
The mean and standard deviation are:

$$\mu = 52.5, \quad \sigma = 10$$

steps.title: Deduce mean and standard deviation
steps.content:
To solve this problem, we first need to find the mean ($\mu$) and standard deviation ($\sigma$). 

From the question, we are given two probabilities:
- $P(T < 45) = 0.2266$
- $P(T > 60) = 0.2266$

Notice that the probabilities are equal for the lower and upper tails. Because the normal distribution is symmetric, the mean $\mu$ lies exactly halfway between these two time values.

$$\mu = \frac{45 + 60}{2} = 52.5$$

To find $\sigma$, we find the Z-score corresponding to the lower tail probability of $0.2266$. Using an inverse normal function, $P(Z < z) = 0.2266$ gives $z \approx -0.75$.

Using the standardization formula $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$:
$$-0.75 = \frac{45 - 52.5}{\sigma}$$
$$-0.75 = \frac{-7.5}{\sigma}$$
$$\sigma = 10$$

steps.title: Identify the required probability
steps.content:
We are asked to calculate the probability that the commuter needs to take a taxi. The question states that this happens if the commute takes more than 65 minutes.

We need to find:
$$P(T > 65)$$

highlights.text: If the commute takes more than 65 minutes, the commuter takes a taxi

steps.title: Standardize the time value
steps.content:
To use the standard normal distribution, we convert the value $T = 65$ into a Z-score using the formula $Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$.

Substitute $x = 65$, $\mu = 52.5$, and $\sigma = 10$:

$$Z = \frac{65 - 52.5}{10}$$

$$Z = \frac{12.5}{10}$$

$$Z = 1.25$$

This means a commute of 65 minutes is 1.25 standard deviations above the mean.

steps.title: Calculate the probability
steps.content:
Now we calculate the probability that the Z-score is greater than 1.25.

$$P(T > 65) = P(Z > 1.25)$$

Using a calculator or standard normal table:

$$\begin{aligned} P(Z > 1.25) &= 1 - P(Z < 1.25) \\ P(Z > 1.25) &\approx 1 - 0.8944 \\ P(Z > 1.25) &\approx 0.1056 \end{aligned}$$

The probability that the commuter needs to take a taxi is **0.1056** (or 10.6%).

steps.title: Analyze the question requirements
steps.content:
The question asks for the probability that the commute took less than 50 minutes, *given* that the commuter arrived on time. The commuter leaves at 07:00 and must arrive by 08:00, so "on time" corresponds to a commute time $T < 60$.

This is a conditional probability problem where we need to find:
$$P(T < 50 \mid T < 60)$$

highlights.text: commuter must arrive by 08:00 to be on time, leaving home at 07:00

steps.title: Select the conditional probability formula
steps.content:
From the **Statistics** section of the data booklet, we use the formula for conditional probability:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

Let event $A$ be $T \lt 50$ and event $B$ be $T \lt 60$. Since any time less than 50 minutes is automatically less than 60 minutes, the intersection is:

$$A \cap B = T \lt 50$$

The formula simplifies to:

$$P(T \lt 50 \mid T \lt 60) = \frac{P(T \lt 50)}{P(T \lt 60)}$$

steps.title: Determine distribution parameters
steps.content:
The question provides symmetrical tail probabilities. Since $P(T < 45) = P(T > 60) = 0.2266$, the normal distribution is centered exactly between $45$ and $60$.

Mean $\mu$:
$$\mu = \frac{45 + 60}{2} = 52.5$$

Standard deviation $\sigma$: The probability $P(T < 45) = 0.2266$ corresponds to a $z$-score of approximately $-0.75$. Using $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$:
$$-0.75 = \frac{45 - 52.5}{\sigma} \implies \sigma = \frac{-7.5}{-0.75} = 10$$

So, $T \sim N(52.5, 10^2)$.

steps.title: Calculate the denominator
steps.content:
We need $P(T < 60)$ for the denominator. We are given $P(T > 60) = 0.2266$. Using the complement rule:

$$P(T < 60) = 1 - P(T > 60)$$
$$P(T < 60) = 1 - 0.2266 = 0.7734$$

steps.title: Identify the conditional probability
steps.content:
The question asks for the probability that the commute took less than 50 minutes, **given** that the commuter arrives on time ($T \le 60$). We use the conditional probability formula from the data booklet:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

Let $A$ be the event $T \lt 50$ and $B$ be the event $T \le 60$. Since $T \lt 50$ is inside the range $T \le 60$, the intersection is:

$$A \cap B = T \lt 50$$

$$P(T \lt 50 \mid T \le 60) = \frac{P(T \lt 50)}{P(T \le 60)}$$

steps.title: Find the mean
steps.content:
First, we need to find the parameters of the normal distribution: the mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$.

Notice that the probabilities for the tails are equal:
$$\begin{aligned} P(T \lt 45) &= 0.2266 \\ P(T \gt 60) &= 0.2266 \end{aligned}$$

Because the normal distribution is symmetric, the mean $\mu$ must be exactly in the middle of these two time values.

$$\mu = \frac{45 + 60}{2} = 52.5$$

steps.title: Find the standard deviation
steps.content:
Next, use the given probability to find the standard deviation $\sigma$. We can use the inverse normal function to find the $z$-score corresponding to the area to the left of 45 minutes.

$$P(T \lt 45) = 0.2266$$

Using a calculator, the $z$-score for an area of 0.2266 is approximately $-0.75$.

Now, apply the standardization formula $$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$ to solve for $\sigma$:

$$\begin{aligned} -0.75 &= \frac{45 - \mu}{\sigma} \\ -0.75 &= \frac{-7.5}{\sigma} \\ \sigma &= \frac{-7.5}{-0.75} \\ \sigma &= 10 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate expected number of days
steps.content:
The number of days the commuter takes a taxi follows a Binomial distribution $X \sim B(n, p)$, where $n$ is the total number of days and $p$ is the probability of taking a taxi on any given day.

Given:
- $n = 240$ days
- $p = 0.1056$

Use the expected value formula from the data booklet:

$$E(X) = np$$

Substitute the values:

$$E(X) = 240 \times 0.1056 = 25.344$$

The expected number of days is **25.3** (to 3 significant figures).

$$\mu = \frac{45 + 60}{2} = 52.5$$

steps.title: Set up standardization equations
steps.content:
From the calculator, we get $z_1 \approx -0.75$ and $z_2 \approx 0.75$. Using the standardization formula $$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$, we set up simultaneous equations for the two known $x$ values ($45$ and $60$):

$$\begin{aligned} -0.75 &= \frac{45 - \mu}{\sigma} \Rightarrow 45 = \mu - 0.75\sigma \\ 0.75 &= \frac{60 - \mu}{\sigma} \Rightarrow 60 = \mu + 0.75\sigma \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for the mean
steps.content:
To find $\mu$, we can add the two equations together to eliminate $\sigma$:

$$\begin{aligned} (45) + (60) &= (\mu - 0.75\sigma) + (\mu + 0.75\sigma) \\ 105 &= 2\mu \\ \mu &= 52.5 \end{aligned}$$

This tells us the normal curve is centered at $52.5$.

- Attempt to find z-scores: $\frac{60-\mu}{\sigma}=\Phi^{-1}(0.20)=-0.8416$, $\frac{85-\mu}{\sigma}=\Phi^{-1}(0.75)=0.6745$ <marks>M1</marks> <marks>A1</marks> <marks>A1</marks>
- Set up simultaneous equations: $60-\mu=-0.8416 \sigma$, $85-\mu=0.6745 \sigma$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Solve for parameters: $\mu=73.9, \sigma=16.5$ <marks>A1</marks> <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content:
Let $S$ be the random variable for the test scores, where $S \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. We need to find the unknown mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$.

The question gives us two probabilities corresponding to specific scores:
1. $P(S < 60) = 0.20$
2. $P(S < 85) = 0.75$

Since we have two unknowns, we will need to set up two simultaneous equations using the standard normal distribution.

steps.title: Find the first z-score
steps.content:
To standardize these values, we first need to find the $z$-score on the standard normal curve $Z \sim N(0, 1)$ that corresponds to the given probabilities. We use the inverse normal function on your calculator.

For $P(S < 60) = 0.20$, we find $z_1$ such that $P(Z < z_1) = 0.20$:

$$z_1 = \Phi^{-1}(0.20) \approx -0.8416$$

gdc.instructions: Go to Statistics/Distribution mode. Select Inverse Normal. Set Area = 0.2, $\mu = 0$, $\sigma = 1$.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR) > 3:invNorm. Enter area: 0.2, $\mu$: 0, $\sigma$: 1.

calculator.description: Find the z-score for a left-tail area of 0.20

steps.title: Find the second z-score
steps.content:
Similarly, for $P(S < 85) = 0.75$, we find the $z$-score $z_2$ such that $P(Z < z_2) = 0.75$:

$$z_2 = \Phi^{-1}(0.75) \approx 0.6745$$

gdc.instructions: Go to Statistics/Distribution mode. Select Inverse Normal. Set Area = 0.75, $\mu = 0$, $\sigma = 1$.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR) > 3:invNorm. Enter area: 0.75, $\mu$: 0, $\sigma$: 1.

calculator.description: Find the z-score for a left-tail area of 0.75

steps.title: Set up simultaneous equations
steps.content:
Using the standardization formula $$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$, we can write an equation for each situation:

1. For $x = 60$ and $z = -0.8416$:
   $$-0.8416 = \frac{60 - \mu}{\sigma} \implies 60 - \mu = -0.8416\sigma$$

2. For $x = 85$ and $z = 0.6745$:
   $$0.6745 = \frac{85 - \mu}{\sigma} \implies 85 - \mu = 0.6745\sigma$$

steps.title: Solve for standard deviation
Solve for mean
steps.content:
Substitute the value of $\sigma$ back into either equation to find $\mu$. Using the second equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu &= 85 - 0.6745\sigma \\
\mu &= 85 - 0.6745(16.489...) \\
\mu &\approx 85 - 11.122 \\
\mu &\approx 73.878...
\end{aligned}
$$

ti84.instructions: Use the stored answer for sigma: 85 - 0.6745 * Ans

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to 3 significant figures as is standard in IB exams:

Mean $\mu = 73.9$
Standard deviation $\sigma = 16.5$

steps.title: Solve for k
steps.content:
Substitute the values into the equation:

$$1.645 = \frac{k - 73.9}{16.5}$$

Multiply both sides by $16.5$ and add $73.9$ to solve for $k$:

$$\begin{aligned} k - 73.9 &= 1.645 \times 16.5 \\ k &= 73.9 + 27.1425 \\ k &\approx 101.04 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify distribution parameters

First, identify the mean ($\mu$) and standard deviation ($\sigma$) from the question. 

The question gives the mean directly, but provides the **variance** ($\sigma^2$) instead of the standard deviation. We must take the square root of the variance to find $\sigma$.

$$\begin{aligned} \mu &= 165 \\ \sigma &= \sqrt{64} = 8 \end{aligned}$$

To find the probability using the standardization method, we convert the height $X = 157$ into a Z-score (standard score). This tells us how many standard deviations the value is from the mean.

The formula for the Z-score is:

This means we are looking for the probability $P(Z \le -1)$.

steps.title: Identify distribution parameters
steps.content:
First, let's identify the parameters of the normal distribution given in the question. We are given the mean $\mu$ and the variance $\sigma^2$.

$$\mu = 165$$

To find the standard deviation $\sigma$, we take the square root of the variance:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{64} = 8$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate square root of 64

ti84.instructions: Calculate square root of 64

steps.title: Standardize the probability statement
steps.content:
The question asks for $k$ where the probability is centered around the mean. We can convert the random variable $H$ to the standard normal variable $Z$ using the formula $Z = \frac{H - \mu}{\sigma}$.

The probability statement is:

$$P(165-k < H < 165+k) = 0.60$$

We convert the upper bound $(165+k)$ to a Z-score:

$$z = \frac{(165+k) - 165}{8} = \frac{k}{8}$$

Due to symmetry, the lower bound converts to $-\frac{k}{8}$. So our standardized equation becomes:

$$P\left(-\frac{k}{8} < Z < \frac{k}{8}\right) = 0.60$$

steps.title: Find cumulative probability
steps.content:
To find the z-score using a calculator, we usually need the cumulative probability (area to the left). 

The central area is $0.60$. Since the normal distribution is symmetric and the total area is $1$, the remaining area in the two tails is $1 - 0.60 = 0.40$.

Each tail contains half of this remaining area:

$$\text{Area in left tail} = \frac{0.40}{2} = 0.20$$

Therefore, the cumulative probability to the left of our upper bound $z = \frac{k}{8}$ is the central area plus the left tail:

$$P\left(Z < \frac{k}{8}\right) = 0.60 + 0.20 = 0.80$$

steps.title: Calculate the z-score
steps.content:
Now we use the inverse normal function on the calculator to find the z-score that corresponds to a cumulative probability of $0.80$.

Using a calculator, we find:

$$z \approx 0.8416$$

steps.title: Solve for k
steps.content:
Finally, we set our standardized expression equal to the calculated z-score and solve for $k$:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{k}{8} &= 0.8416 \\ k &= 0.8416 \times 8 \\ k &\approx 6.73 \end{aligned}$$

(Note: Using the rounded value $0.842$ gives $6.74$, which is also accepted.)

steps.title: Determine cumulative probability
steps.content:
The question asks for the central region where the probability is $0.60$. The normal distribution is symmetric, so the remaining probability of $1 - 0.60 = 0.40$ is split equally between the two tails.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Area of left tail} &= \frac{1 - 0.60}{2} = 0.20 \\ \text{Cumulative Area} &= 0.60 + 0.20 = 0.80 \end{aligned}$$

To find the upper bound $(165+k)$, we use the cumulative area to its left, which is $0.80$.

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
We need to find the value of $k$ such that the probability of the height $H$ falling between $165-k$ and $165+k$ is $0.60$. 

Using the normal cumulative distribution function (normalcdf) on your calculator, we can write this relationship as an equation with $k$ as the unknown variable:

$$\text{normalcdf}(\text{lower: } 165-k, \, \text{upper: } 165+k, \, \mu: 165, \, \sigma: 8) = 0.60$$

steps.title: Solve using technology
steps.content:
Since $k$ is inside the boundary limits of the normal distribution function, the most efficient way to solve this is using the graphing or numeric solver feature of your calculator. 

We define a function for the probability area in terms of $x$ (which represents $k$) and find where it equals $0.60$.

steps.title: Scale the boundary value
steps.content:
Using the **Standard Deviation Scaling** approach, we determine the exact position of the boundary value $157$ relative to the mean in terms of standard deviations.

$$\text{Distance from mean} = 157 - 165 = -8$$

Since $\sigma = 8$, this distance corresponds to exactly one standard deviation below the mean:

$$157 = \mu - 1\sigma$$

This is a critical geometric feature: for a normal distribution curve, the points $\mu \pm \sigma$ are the **inflection points** where the curve changes from convex to concave.

steps.content: First, we identify the parameters of the normal distribution given in the question. We have a mean $\mu = 75$ and a standard deviation $\sigma = 6$.

steps.title: Set up probability equation
steps.content:
The question asks for the value of $a$ such that the probability of $Y$ being less than $a$ is $0.85$. We can write this as:

$$P(Y < a) = 0.85$$

Since we know the probability (area under the curve) and need to find the boundary value $a$, we will use the **Inverse Normal** function on the calculator.

steps.content:
We can input the mean and standard deviation directly into the calculator's Inverse Normal function to solve for $a$ without needing to standardize first.

**Parameters:**
*   **Area:** $0.85$ (this is the probability to the left of $a$)
*   **$\mu$ (Mean):** $75$
*   **$\sigma$ (Standard Deviation):** $6$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Using the calculator, we find:

$$a \approx 81.218$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, the value is:

$$a = 81.2$$

steps.title: Standardize the variable
steps.content:
To use the standard normal distribution, we first need to convert the random variable $Y$ to the standard normal variable $Z$. We use the standardization formula:

$$Z = \frac{Y - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Substituting the given mean $\mu=75$ and standard deviation $\sigma=6$, the probability statement becomes:

$$P(Y < a) = P\left(Z < \frac{a - 75}{6}\right) = 0.85$$

steps.title: Find the z-score
steps.content:
Now we need to find the specific z-score, let's call it $z$, such that the cumulative probability is $0.85$. In other words, we need to solve $P(Z < z) = 0.85$.

Using the inverse normal function on your calculator, we find:

$$z \approx 1.0364$$

steps.content:
We equate our standardized expression from Step 0 to the z-score found in Step 1:

$$\frac{a - 75}{6} = 1.0364$$

Now, solve for $a$:

$$\begin{aligned} a - 75 &= 1.0364 \times 6 \\ a &= 75 + 6.2184 \\ a &\approx 81.218... \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content: First, let's identify the parameters given in the question. We know the distribution is normal with mean $\mu = 10$. We are interested in the probability where the diameter $D$ is less than $9.5$, which corresponds to the lower tail of the distribution.

highlights.text: probability that a rod has a diameter less than $9.5 \text{ mm}$ is $0.00135$

steps.title: Find the z-score
steps.content:
Since we know the probability but not the standard deviation, we use the **Standardization Approach**. We first find the $z$-score that corresponds to a cumulative probability of $0.00135$ using the inverse normal function on your calculator.

We use the standard normal distribution parameters: mean $\mu = 0$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 1$.

gdc.instructions: Go to Statistics > Distributions > Normal > Inverse Normal. Set Area = 0.00135, μ = 0, σ = 1.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR) > 3:invNorm. Enter area = 0.00135, μ = 0, σ = 1.

steps.title: State the z-score
steps.content:
The calculator gives a $z$-score of exactly $-3.0$.

$$z = -3.0$$

steps.title: Apply standardization formula
steps.content:
Now we use the standardization formula, which relates the $z$-score, the value $x$, the mean $\mu$, and the standard deviation $\sigma$:

$$Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Substitute our known values ($z = -3$, $x = 9.5$, $\mu = 10$) into the equation:

$$-3 = \frac{9.5 - 10}{\sigma}$$

steps.title: Solve for sigma
steps.content:
Rearrange the equation to solve for $\sigma$:

$$\begin{aligned} -3\sigma &= 9.5 - 10 \\ -3\sigma &= -0.5 \\ \sigma &= \frac{-0.5}{-3} \\ \sigma &\approx 0.167 \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate -0.5 divided by -3

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content: The standard deviation is approximately **0.167 mm** (or $0.166...$).

steps.content:
Before we can calculate the probability of acceptance, we need to find the standard deviation, $\sigma$, using the information given about rejected rods.

We know that the probability of a diameter less than $9.5 \text{ mm}$ is $0.00135$. In a standard normal distribution, a tail probability of $0.00135$ corresponds almost exactly to a Z-score of $-3$.

Using the standardization formula $$Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$:

$$\begin{aligned} -3 &= \frac{9.5 - 10}{\sigma} \\ -3 &= \frac{-0.5}{\sigma} \\ \sigma &= \frac{-0.5}{-3} \approx 0.167 \text{ (or } \frac{1}{6}\text{)} \end{aligned}$$

For the upper bound ($x = 10.2$):

steps.title: Identify conditional probability

The question asks for the probability that the diameter is less than $9.8$ mm **given that** the rod is rejected. This is a conditional probability problem. Let event $A$ be "diameter $< 9.8$" and event $B$ be "rod is rejected".

We use the conditional probability formula:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

steps.title: Find the standard deviation
steps.content:
To calculate specific probabilities, we first need the standard deviation $\sigma$. We are given that $P(D < 9.5) = 0.00135$. Using the inverse normal distribution, this probability corresponds to a $z$-score of $z = -3$.

Using the standardization formula:

$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} \Rightarrow -3 = \frac{9.5 - 10}{\sigma}$$

Solving for $\sigma$:

$$\sigma = \frac{-0.5}{-3} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667 \text{ mm}$$

steps.title: Calculate numerator probability
steps.content:
Now we calculate the probability for the numerator, $P(A \cap B)$.

* Event $A$: $D < 9.8$
* Event $B$: Rejected ($D < 9.8$ or $D > 10.2$)

The intersection $A \cap B$ is simply $D < 9.8$. We find the $z$-score for $D = 9.8$:

$$z = \frac{9.8 - 10}{1/6} = -1.2$$

Using the normal cumulative distribution function:

$$P(D < 9.8) = P(Z < -1.2) \approx 0.115$$

steps.title: Calculate denominator probability
steps.content:
Next, we find the total probability of rejection, $P(B)$. A rod is rejected if $D < 9.8$ or $D > 10.2$. Due to the symmetry of the normal distribution around $\mu = 10$, the upper tail probability is equal to the lower tail probability:

$$P(D > 10.2) = P(D < 9.8) \approx 0.115$$

Therefore, the total probability of rejection is:

$$P(\text{rejected}) = 0.115 + 0.115 = 0.230$$

highlights.text: Rods with diameters between 9.8 and 10.2 mm are accepted; others are rejected.

steps.title: Apply conditional formula
steps.content:
Finally, apply the conditional probability formula using our calculated values:

$$\begin{aligned} P(D < 9.8 \mid \text{rejected}) &= \frac{P(D < 9.8)}{P(\text{rejected})} \\ &= \frac{0.115}{0.230} \\ &= 0.5 \end{aligned}$$

This result makes intuitive sense: because the rejection region is symmetric, if a rod is rejected, it is equally likely to be too small or too large.

steps.title: Setup conditional probability
steps.content:
We need to find the probability that a rod has a diameter less than $9.8$ mm, *given* that it is rejected. We apply the conditional probability formula from the **Statistics** section of the data booklet:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

Let $A$ be the event $D < 9.8$ and $B$ be the event that the rod is rejected.

steps.title: Analyze rejection symmetry
steps.content:
The question states the mean is $\mu=10$ mm and rods are accepted between $9.8$ and $10.2$ mm. Notice that the rejection region is perfectly symmetric around the mean:

*   **Lower boundary:** $10 - 9.8 = 0.2$ mm below the mean.
*   **Upper boundary:** $10.2 - 10 = 0.2$ mm above the mean.

A rod is rejected if it falls into the left tail ($D < 9.8$) or the right tail ($D > 10.2$).

steps.title: Relate tail probabilities
steps.content:
Due to the symmetry of the normal distribution, the probability of being in the left tail ($D < 9.8$) is exactly equal to the probability of being in the right tail ($D > 10.2$).

Let $p$ be the probability of one tail. Then:
*   $P(D < 9.8) = p$
*   $P(D > 10.2) = p$

The total probability of being rejected is the sum of these two mutually exclusive tails:
$$P(\text{rejected}) = p + p = 2p$$

steps.title: Solve the probability
steps.content:
Now we substitute these values back into our conditional probability formula.

The intersection event ($D < 9.8$ *and* rejected) is simply the left tail ($D < 9.8$), because all rods in the left tail are rejected.

$$\begin{aligned} P(D < 9.8 \mid \text{rejected}) &= \frac{P(D < 9.8)}{P(\text{rejected})} \\ &= \frac{p}{2p} \\ &= 0.5 \end{aligned}$$

This method gives the exact answer without needing to calculate the specific areas (like $0.115$ or $0.230$) shown in the markscheme.

steps.title: Calculate acceptance probability
steps.content:
First, we need to find the probability $p$ that a single rod is accepted. From the question, we know the diameters are normally distributed with $\mu = 10$. Using the condition $P(D < 9.5) = 0.00135$, we can find the standard deviation.

The $z$-score corresponding to a probability of $0.00135$ is approximately $-3$ (since $1 - 0.99865 = 0.00135$).

$$\begin{aligned} z &= \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} \\ -3 &= \frac{9.5 - 10}{\sigma} \\ -3\sigma &= -0.5 \\ \sigma &= \frac{1}{6} \end{aligned}$$

The accepted range is $9.8$ to $10.2$ mm. Converting these to $z$-scores:

$$z_1 = \frac{9.8 - 10}{1/6} = -1.2$$

$$z_2 = \frac{10.2 - 10}{1/6} = 1.2$$

Using the normal CDF on your calculator for $z \in [-1.2, 1.2]$:

$$p = P(9.8 \leq D \leq 10.2) \approx 0.76986 \approx 0.770$$

steps.title: Identify Binomial Distribution
steps.content:
Since we are looking at the number of accepted rods out of a fixed sample size of $1000$, and each rod is either accepted or rejected independently with constant probability $p \approx 0.770$, this is a Binomial distribution.

Let $X$ be the number of accepted rods. We define the distribution as:

$$X \sim B(1000, 0.770)$$

steps.title: Set up the probability
steps.content:
The question asks for the probability that **more than 850** rods are accepted. In probability notation, this is:

$$P(X > 850)$$

Most graphing calculators use the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF), which calculates $P(X \leq k)$. To use the calculator, we rewrite the expression:

$$P(X > 850) = 1 - P(X \leq 850)$$

highlights.text: probability that more than $850$ are accepted

steps.title: Calculate using GDC
steps.content:
Now, use the **binomial CDF** function on your calculator. You will need the number of trials $n=1000$, the probability $p=0.770$, and the upper bound value $x=850$.

Calculate:

$$1 - \text{binomcdf}(1000, 0.770, 850)$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator gives a very small probability. Writing it in standard form:

$$P(X > 850) \approx 1.13 \times 10^{-10}$$

parts.content: Find the probability that a randomly selected bulb lasts between 1200 and 1700 hours.

steps.title: Identify the distribution parameters
steps.content: First, we identify the parameters of the normal distribution given in the question. We are told the lifetime $T$ is normally distributed with a mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$.

steps.title: State the probability required
steps.content:
We need to find the probability that a bulb lasts between 1200 and 1700 hours. This can be written as:

$$P(1200 < T < 1700)$$

steps.title: Calculate using Normal CDF
steps.content:
Since we are using the **Direct GDC Approach**, we can input the lower bound, upper bound, mean, and standard deviation directly into the calculator's Normal Cumulative Distribution Function (Normal CDF). 

We set:
- **Lower Bound:** $1200$
- **Upper Bound:** $1700$
- **Mean ($\mu$):** $1500$
- **Standard Deviation ($\sigma$):** $200$

gdc.instructions:
Go to Statistics > Distributions > Normal > Ncd
Set Data: Variable
Lower: 1200
Upper: 1700
σ: 200
μ: 1500
Execute

ti84.instructions:
Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR) > 2:normalcdf
Enter lower: 1200
Enter upper: 1700
Enter μ: 1500
Enter σ: 200
Paste and Enter

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator gives the probability approximately as $0.77453...$

Rounding to 4 decimal places (as shown in the markscheme) or 3 significant figures (standard IB practice):

$$P(1200 < T < 1700) = 0.7745$$

steps.title: Identify the parameters
steps.content: First, we identify the parameters of the normal distribution given in the question. We have the mean $\mu$ and the standard deviation $\sigma$.

steps.title: State the standardization formula
steps.content:
To use the standard normal distribution, we convert the $x$-values (hours) into $Z$-scores using the standardization formula:

$$Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

This tells us how many standard deviations a value is away from the mean.

steps.title: Calculate the lower Z-score
steps.content:
Substitute the lower boundary $x = 1200$ into the formula:

$$Z_1 = \frac{1200 - 1500}{200} = \frac{-300}{200} = -1.5$$

So, 1200 hours is 1.5 standard deviations below the mean.

steps.title: Calculate the upper Z-score
steps.content:
Substitute the upper boundary $x = 1700$ into the formula:

$$Z_2 = \frac{1700 - 1500}{200} = \frac{200}{200} = 1$$

So, 1700 hours is 1 standard deviation above the mean.

steps.title: Rewrite the probability
steps.content:
We can now rewrite the probability in terms of the standard normal variable $Z$:

$$P(1200 < T < 1700) = P(-1.5 < Z < 1)$$

This step is crucial for showing your method and often earns a method mark (M1).

steps.title: Calculate the probability
steps.content: Use your calculator to find the probability (area under the curve) for the standard normal distribution $N(0, 1)$ between the limits $-1.5$ and $1$.

gdc.instructions:
Go to Statistics/Distribution mode.
Select Normal Cumulative (Ncd).
Set Lower: -1.5, Upper: 1, σ: 1, μ: 0.

ti84.instructions:
Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR) > 2:normalcdf.
Enter lower: -1.5, upper: 1, μ: 0, σ: 1.
Paste and Enter.

calculator.description: Calculate area under standard normal curve

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The calculator gives a value of approximately $0.7745$.

$$P(1200 < T < 1700) = 0.775$$ (to 3 significant figures)

steps.title: Calculate single bulb probability
steps.content:
First, we calculate the probability that a single bulb lasts more than 1300 hours using the normal distribution $T \sim N(1500, 200^2)$.

Standardize the value 1300 to find the Z-score:
$$Z = \frac{1300 - 1500}{200} = -1$$

The probability is:
$$P(T > 1300) = P(Z > -1) = 0.8413$$

steps.title: Define binomial distribution
steps.content:
Now we consider the batch of 50 bulbs. Let $Y$ be the number of bulbs that last more than 1300 hours.

Since there is a fixed number of trials and a constant probability of success $p = 0.8413$, this follows a binomial distribution:
$$Y \sim B(50, 0.8413)$$

steps.title: Apply binomial complement rule
steps.content:
We need to find the probability that **at least 45** bulbs last more than 1300 hours, which is $P(Y \geq 45)$.

Using the complement rule, we can rewrite this using the cumulative probability $P(Y \leq 44)$:
$$P(Y \geq 45) = 1 - P(Y \leq 44)$$

steps.title: Calculate final probability
steps.content:
Using the binomial cumulative distribution function (CDF) on your calculator:

$$1 - P(Y \leq 44) = 1 - 0.8259... = 0.174$$

steps.title: Probability for a single bulb
steps.content:
First, we model the lifetime of a single bulb $T$ using the normal distribution given in the question. We need the probability that a bulb lasts **more than 1300 hours**.

Given: $T \sim N(1500, 200^2)$

Using the standardized normal variable $Z$:
$$Z = \frac{T - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{1300 - 1500}{200} = \frac{-200}{200} = -1$$

So, the probability for one bulb is:
$$p = P(T > 1300) = P(Z > -1)$$

steps.title: Express the required probability
steps.content:
The question asks for the probability that **at least 45 bulbs** last longer than 1300 hours. This means we are looking for $P(Y \geq 45)$.

Using the direct summation method, we add the probabilities for all outcomes from 45 up to 50:

$$P(Y \geq 45) = P(Y=45) + P(Y=46) + P(Y=47) + P(Y=48) + P(Y=49) + P(Y=50)$$

steps.title: Calculate the final probability
steps.content:
Using the Binomial Cumulative Distribution function (Binomial CD) on your calculator, find the probability for the interval $45 \leq Y \leq 50$.

Note: If your calculator only supports cumulative probability from 0 (like older TI-84s), you can calculate $1 - P(Y \leq 44)$.

$$P(Y \geq 45) \approx 0.174$$

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
First, let's identify the parameters given in the question. We have a normal distribution with a known mean $\mu$ and an unknown standard deviation $\sigma$.

We are given:
- Mean $\mu = 50$ cm
- The probability that a rod is shorter than $48$ cm is $0.0228$. Mathematically, this is $P(L \lt 48) = 0.0228$.

highlights.text: probability that a rod is shorter than 48 \text{ cm} is 0.0228

steps.title: Find the z-score
steps.content:
This question requires finding an unknown parameter ($\sigma$) given a probability. This is a classic **Inverse Normal** problem covered in **Topic SL 4.12**.

We need to find the standardized z-score that corresponds to the cumulative probability of $0.0228$. This represents the value on the standard normal curve $Z \sim N(0, 1)$ where the area to the left is $0.0228$.

Using your calculator's inverse normal function:

$$z = \Phi^{-1}(0.0228) = -2.0$$

gdc.instructions:
Go to Statistics/Distribution > Norm > InvN.
Set Tail: Left, Area: 0.0228, μ: 0, σ: 1.

ti84.instructions:
Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR) > 3:invNorm.
Enter area: 0.0228, μ: 0, σ: 1.

calculator.description: Calculate the inverse normal to find the z-score.

steps.title: Apply standardization formula
steps.content:
Now we use the standardization formula, which relates the specific value $x$, the mean $\mu$, the standard deviation $\sigma$, and the z-score $z$:

$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Substitute the known values ($z = -2$, $x = 48$, $\mu = 50$) into the equation:

$$-2 = \frac{48 - 50}{\sigma}$$

steps.title: Solve for sigma
steps.content:
Finally, we solve the equation for $\sigma$.

$$\begin{aligned} -2 &= \frac{-2}{\sigma} \\ -2\sigma &= -2 \\ \sigma &= 1 \end{aligned}$$

So, the standard deviation is $1.0$ cm.

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
We are working with a normal distribution variable $L$ representing rod lengths. Identify the known parameters and the unknown variable:

*   **Mean**: $\mu = 50$
*   **Condition**: The probability of a length less than $48$ is $0.0228$, which we write as $P(L \lt 48) = 0.0228$
*   **Unknown**: Standard deviation $\sigma$

highlights.text: probability that a rod is shorter than 48 \text{ cm} is 0.0228

steps.title: Set up the solver equation
steps.content:
To find the unknown standard deviation $\sigma$ directly, we set up an equation using the calculator's **cumulative normal distribution** function (often called `normalcdf` or `normCdf`). 

The general syntax is:

$$P(\text{lower} \lt L \lt \text{upper}) = \text{normalcdf}(\text{lower}, \text{upper}, \mu, \sigma)$$

Substituting our values:

$$\text{normalcdf}(-10^{99}, 48, 50, x) = 0.0228$$

Where:
*   Lower bound is effectively negative infinity (use $-10^{99}$ or $-1\text{E}99$)
*   Upper bound is $48$
*   Mean is $50$
*   $x$ represents the unknown $\sigma$

steps.title: Solve using GDC
steps.content: Enter this equation into your calculator's **Numerical Solver** (or Equation Solver) to find the value of $x$. Ensure you set a positive initial guess for $x$ (since standard deviation must be positive), such as $x=5$.

2. Press [Menu] > 3: Algebra > 1: Numerical Solve (nSolve).
3. Enter the equation: nSolve(normCdf(-∞, 48, 50, s) = 0.0228, s)
   (Find normCdf under the distribution menu).
4. Press [Enter].

steps.title: Find the z-score
steps.content:
Using the inverse normal distribution on your calculator, find the $z$-score corresponding to a left-tail area (probability) of $0.0668$.

$$
P(Z < z) = 0.0668 \implies z = -1.5
$$

Use the standardization formula $$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$ to set up an equation. Substitute the values $z = -1.5$, $x = 100$, and $\mu = 120$:

$$
-1.5 = \frac{100 - 120}{\sigma}
$$

steps.title: Solve for sigma
steps.content:
Simplify the numerator and rearrange the equation to solve for $\sigma$:

$$-1.5 = \frac{-20}{\sigma}$$

Multiply both sides by $\sigma$ and divide by $-1.5$:

$$\sigma = \frac{-20}{-1.5}$$

steps.title: Identify conditional probability
steps.content:
We need to find the probability that a task is flagged for review ($S > 150$) given that it takes more than 110 seconds ($S > 110$).

Using the conditional probability formula from the data booklet:
$$ P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} $$

Substituting our events:
$$ P(S > 150 \mid S > 110) = \frac{P(S > 150 \cap S > 110)}{P(S > 110)} $$

Since any task taking longer than 150 seconds automatically takes longer than 110 seconds, the intersection is just $S > 150$:
$$ P(S > 150 \mid S > 110) = \frac{P(S > 150)}{P(S > 110)} $$

steps.title: Calculate standard deviation
steps.content:
To calculate these probabilities, we first need the standard deviation $\sigma$. We know the mean $\mu = 120$ and that $P(S < 100) = 0.0668$.

Using the inverse normal function on the calculator, we find the $z$-score associated with a probability of $0.0668$:

$$z = \text{invNorm}(0.0668) \approx -1.50$$

Using the standardization formula $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$:

$$-1.50 = \frac{100 - 120}{\sigma}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{-20}{-1.50} \approx 13.333$$

First, we need to find the standard deviation $\sigma$. We are given that $\mu = 120$ and the probability of a task taking less than 100 seconds is 0.0668.

We know that $$P(S < 100) = 0.0668$$. Using the inverse normal function on your calculator (or a Z-table), we find the Z-score that corresponds to a left-tail probability of 0.0668 is approximately $-1.5$.

steps.title: Setup conditional probability
steps.content:
The question asks for the probability that a task is flagged (takes $S > 150$) **given that** it takes $S > 110$. We use the conditional probability formula from the data booklet:

$$ P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} $$

Let $A$ be the event $S > 150$ and $B$ be the event $S > 110$.

The intersection $A \cap B$ means the task is **both** greater than 150 and greater than 110. Since any value greater than 150 is automatically greater than 110, the intersection is simply $S > 150$.

So our formula becomes:
$$ P(S > 150 \mid S > 110) = \frac{P(S > 150)}{P(S > 110)} $$

$$

We need the probability $P(Z > -0.75)$. Using the symmetry of the normal distribution, this is equal to the area to the left of $Z = 0.75$, or found directly via calculator:

$$

steps.title: Find probability of flagging

First, we need to find the probability $p$ that a single task is flagged ($S > 150$). 

We are given that time $S$ is normally distributed with mean $\mu = 120$. We are also given that $P(S < 100) = 0.0668$. Using the inverse normal function on your calculator, we can find the standard deviation $\sigma$.

$$\begin{aligned} P(S < 100) &= 0.0668 \\ z_1 &= \text{invNorm}(0.0668) \approx -1.5 \\ -1.5 &= \frac{100 - 120}{\sigma} \\ \sigma &= \frac{-20}{-1.5} = 13.33\dots \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} z_2 &= \frac{150 - 120}{13.33\dots} = 2.25 \\ p &= P(Z > 2.25) \approx 0.0122 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Set up Binomial Distribution
steps.content:
Let $X$ be the number of flagged tasks. Since there are a fixed number of tasks and each has a constant probability of being flagged, $X$ follows a binomial distribution:
$$X \sim B(n, p)$$

Where:
*   $n = 80$ (total tasks)
*   $p = 0.0122$ (probability of a flag)

We need to find the probability that **fewer than 5** tasks are flagged:
$$P(X < 5) = P(X \le 4)$$

steps.title: Identify terms to sum
steps.content:
Using the Summing Individual Probabilities approach, we will calculate the probability for each outcome from 0 to 4 and sum them:

$$ P(X \le 4) = P(X=0) + P(X=1) + P(X=2) + P(X=3) + P(X=4) $$

The formula for a single binomial probability is:
$$ P(X=r) = \binom{n}{r} p^r (1-p)^{n-r} $$

steps.title: Calculate P(X=0)
steps.content:
Calculate the probability of exactly 0 flagged tasks:
$$\begin{aligned} P(X=0) &= \binom{80}{0} (0.0122)^0 (0.9878)^{80} \\ &= 1 \times 1 \times (0.9878)^{80} \\ &\approx 0.3746 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 0.9878^80

ti84.instructions: Calculate 0.9878^80

steps.title: Calculate P(X=1)
steps.content:
Calculate the probability of exactly 1 flagged task:
$$\begin{aligned} P(X=1) &= \binom{80}{1} (0.0122)^1 (0.9878)^{79} \\ &= 80 \times 0.0122 \times (0.9878)^{79} \\ &\approx 0.3701 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate P(X=2)
steps.content:
Calculate the probability of exactly 2 flagged tasks:
$$\begin{aligned} P(X=2) &= \binom{80}{2} (0.0122)^2 (0.9878)^{78} \\ &= 3160 \times (0.0122)^2 \times (0.9878)^{78} \\ &\approx 0.1805 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate P(X=3) and P(X=4)
steps.content:
Calculate the probabilities for 3 and 4 flagged tasks:
$$\begin{aligned} P(X=3) &= \binom{80}{3} (0.0122)^3 (0.9878)^{77} \\ &\approx 0.0580 \\ \\ P(X=4) &= \binom{80}{4} (0.0122)^4 (0.9878)^{76} \\ &\approx 0.0138 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Sum probabilities
steps.content:
Finally, sum the individual probabilities to find the total probability:
$$\begin{aligned} P(X < 5) &= 0.3746 + 0.3701 + 0.1805 + 0.0580 + 0.0138 \\ &= 0.997 \end{aligned}$$

The probability that fewer than 5 tasks are flagged is **0.997**.

parts.content: Find the values of $\mu$ and $\sigma$ (to 3 significant figures).
parts.markscheme:
- **Set** up z-score equations based on percentiles: $P(X > 72) = 0.20 \Rightarrow z_1 \approx 0.8416$ and $P(X < 40) = 0.10 \Rightarrow z_2 \approx -1.2816$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Write** simultaneous equations: $\frac{72-\mu}{\sigma} = 0.8416$ and $\frac{40-\mu}{\sigma} = -1.2816$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Solve** for $\sigma$ by subtraction: $\frac{32}{\sigma} \approx 2.1232 \Rightarrow \sigma \approx 15.1$ <marks>A1</marks>
- **Solve** for $\mu$ by substitution: $\mu \approx 72 - 0.8416(15.1) \approx 59.3$ <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Allow $\mu \in [59.2, 59.4]$ and $\sigma \in [15.0, 15.2]$ with consistent working.</note>

steps.title: Analyze the given probabilities
steps.content:
We are working with a normal distribution $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ where the mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$ are unknown. We are given two conditions:
1. $20\%$ of students scored above 72: $$P(X > 72) = 0.20$$
2. $10\%$ of students scored below 40: $$P(X < 40) = 0.10$$

To solve for the two unknowns, we will first find the standardized $z$-scores corresponding to these probabilities. This relates to **Topic 4.12: Z values and inverse normal**.

steps.title: Find the z-scores
steps.content:
Use the **inverse normal** function on your calculator to find the $z$-scores associated with the cumulative probabilities.

For $P(X > 72) = 0.20$, the area to the *left* is $1 - 0.20 = 0.80$. We find $z_1$ such that $P(Z < z_1) = 0.80$.
For $P(X < 40) = 0.10$, the area to the *left* is $0.10$. We find $z_2$ such that $P(Z < z_2) = 0.10$.

Using a calculator:
$$z_1 \approx 0.8416$$
$$z_2 \approx -1.2816$$

gdc.instructions: Go to Statistics mode > Distributions > Normal > Inverse Normal. Set Area=0.8, μ=0, σ=1.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR) > 3:invNorm. Enter area 0.80, μ=0, σ=1 for the first score.

steps.title: Set up simultaneous equations
steps.content:
Using the standardization formula $$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$, we can set up two linear equations with our values:

1. For $X = 72$ and $z_1 = 0.8416$:
   $$\frac{72 - \mu}{\sigma} = 0.8416 \implies 72 - \mu = 0.8416\sigma$$

2. For $X = 40$ and $z_2 = -1.2816$:
   $$\frac{40 - \mu}{\sigma} = -1.2816 \implies 40 - \mu = -1.2816\sigma$$

steps.title: Solve for sigma
steps.content:
We can solve this system algebraically by eliminating $\mu$. Subtract the second equation from the first:

$$(72 - \mu) - (40 - \mu) = 0.8416\sigma - (-1.2816\sigma)$$

$$32 = (0.8416 + 1.2816)\sigma$$

$$32 = 2.1232\sigma$$

$$\sigma = \frac{32}{2.1232} \approx 15.1$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the value of sigma

ti84.instructions: Calculate the value of sigma

steps.title: Solve for mu
steps.content:
Substitute the value of $\sigma$ back into the first equation to find $\mu$:

$$\mu = 72 - 0.8416(15.0716)$$

$$\mu \approx 72 - 12.68$$

$$\mu \approx 59.3$$

(We use the more precise value of $\sigma \approx 15.0716$ for calculation to maintain accuracy).

steps.title: State the final answers
steps.content:
Rounding to 3 significant figures as requested:

$$\mu = 59.3, \quad \sigma = 15.1$$

1. **Right tail:** $20\%$ of students scored above $72$. This means the area to the *left* of $72$ is $1 - 0.20 = 0.80$.
   $$P(X < 72) = 0.80$$

2. **Left tail:** $10\%$ of students scored below $40$. This is already in the correct form for finding a z-score.
   $$P(X < 40) = 0.10$$

steps.content:
Use the **inverse normal** function on your calculator to find the z-score (standardized score) corresponding to each cumulative probability.

steps.title: Set up standardized equations
steps.content:
Using the standardization formula $$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$, we can set up two equations with our known $x$ values ($72$ and $40$) and the z-scores we just found.

Equation 1:  
$$0.8416 = \frac{72 - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Equation 2:  
$$-1.2816 = \frac{40 - \mu}{\sigma}$$

steps.title: Rearrange for graphing
steps.content:
To solve this graphically, we will rearrange both equations to express the mean ($\mu$) as a linear function of the standard deviation ($\sigma$). Think of $\mu$ as $y$ and $\sigma$ as $x$.

From Equation 1 ($72 - \mu = 0.8416\sigma$):
$$\mu = 72 - 0.8416\sigma$$

From Equation 2 ($40 - \mu = -1.2816\sigma$):
$$\mu = 40 + 1.2816\sigma$$

Now we have a system of two linear equations ready to be plotted.

1.  Enter $Y_1 = 72 - 0.8416X$
2.  Enter $Y_2 = 40 + 1.2816X$
3.  Find the intersection point.

The intersection occurs at approximately $X \approx 15.07$ and $Y \approx 59.32$.

parts.content: The pass mark this year will be set so that the top 35% of candidates pass. Find the pass mark $c$ (to the nearest integer).
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** the z-score for the top 35%: $P(X \ge c) = 0.35 \Rightarrow P(X \le c) = 0.65$. Using inverse normal, $z_{0.65} \approx 0.385$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Calculate** the pass mark: $c = \mu + 0.385\sigma \approx 59.3 + 0.385(15.1) \approx 65.1$ <marks>A1</marks>
- **State** the integer value: $c = 65$

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the required percentile
steps.content:
The question states that the **top 35%** of candidates pass. In a normal distribution, the top 35% corresponds to the area to the right of the pass mark $c$. 

To use the inverse normal function (which typically calculates area from the left), we need the cumulative probability (percentile) below the pass mark:

$$P(X < c) = 1 - 0.35 = 0.65$$

steps.title: Find the z-score
steps.content:
Using the inverse normal distribution with an area of $0.65$, a mean of $0$, and a standard deviation of $1$, we calculate the standardized z-score.

$$z = \Phi^{-1}(0.65) \approx 0.385$$

$$X = \mu + z\sigma$$

Using the distribution parameters derived from the previous part ($\mu \approx 59.3$ and $\sigma \approx 15.1$) and the z-score we just found:

$$c = 59.3 + (0.385)(15.1)$$

parts.content: A school enters $n=80$ candidates. Using your model, estimate the probability that at least 35 of them pass. State any distribution you use and any approximation.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** distribution parameters: $W \sim \mathrm{Bin}(80, 0.35)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Express** the required probability: $P(W \ge 35) = 1 - P(W \le 34)$ <marks>A1</marks>
- **Calculate** the probability using technology: $P(W \ge 35) \approx 0.0654$ <marks>A2</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Accept valid Normal approximation: $\mu_W = 28$, $\sigma_W \approx 4.27$. Continuity correction gives $P(W_{cont} > 34.5) = P(Z > 1.52) \approx 0.064$.</note>

steps.title: Define the random variable
steps.content:
Let $W$ be the number of students who pass the test out of the $n=80$ candidates. Since each student passes or fails independently with a constant probability, we model $W$ as a binomial random variable.

From the context of the question (and consistent with the markscheme), we use the probability of passing determined in previous parts: $$p = 0.35$$

steps.title: State the distribution
steps.content:
The distribution for the number of passing students $W$ is binomial with parameters $n=80$ and $p=0.35$. We write this as:

$$W \sim \text{Bin}(80, 0.35)$$

steps.title: Formulate the probability
steps.content:
We need to find the probability that **at least** 35 students pass. In inequality notation, this is $P(W \ge 35)$.

Most calculators calculate the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) for values **less than or equal to** $x$, i.e., $P(W \le x)$. Therefore, we rewrite the probability using the complement rule:

$$P(W \ge 35) = 1 - P(W \le 34)$$

steps.title: Calculate using technology
steps.content:
Use your graphing calculator's binomial CDF function to find the value.

Calculate $P(W \le 34)$ first, then subtract from 1.

$$P(W \ge 35) = 1 - 0.9346... \approx 0.0654$$

steps.title: Identify the distribution
steps.content:
Let $W$ be the random variable representing the number of candidates who pass the test. This can be modelled by a binomial distribution with $n=80$ candidates. Based on the previous parts of the question, the probability of a single student passing is $p=0.35$.

$$W \sim \text{Bin}(80, 0.35)$$

steps.title: Find normal approximation parameters
steps.content:
Since $n$ is large, we can approximate the binomial distribution using a normal distribution. First, we calculate the mean ($\mu$) and standard deviation ($\sigma$) using the formulas from the data booklet.

$$\begin{aligned} \mu &= np = 80(0.35) = 28 \\ \sigma &= \sqrt{np(1-p)} = \sqrt{80(0.35)(0.65)} = \sqrt{18.2} \approx 4.266 \end{aligned}$$

Both $np=28$ and $n(1-p)=52$ are greater than 5, so the normal approximation is valid.

steps.title: Apply continuity correction
steps.content:
We need to find the probability that **at least 35** candidates pass, which is $P(W \ge 35)$.

When approximating a discrete distribution with a continuous one, we apply a continuity correction of $0.5$. To include the value $35$, we start the lower bound at $34.5$.

$$P(W \ge 35) \approx P(W_{\text{normal}} > 34.5)$$

parts.content: The Head of Department claims that *“a student who scores 15 points above the mean is about 2 standard deviations above average.”* Decide whether this claim is reasonable, justifying quantitatively.
parts.markscheme:
- **Calculate** the z-score for the difference: $z = \frac{15}{\sigma} \approx \frac{15}{15.1} \approx 0.99$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Conclude** that the claim is not reasonable as $z \approx 1$, not 2 <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Retrieve standard deviation
steps.content:
From the calculations in the previous part of the question, we use the standard deviation value:

$$\sigma \approx 15.1$$

The claim discusses a student scoring "15 points above the mean". This corresponds to a difference of:

$$x - \mu = 15$$

steps.title: Calculate the z-score
steps.content:
To test the Head of Department's claim, we calculate the $z$-score for this difference. The $z$-score represents the number of standard deviations a value is from the mean.

Using the formula $$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$, we substitute our values:

$$z = \frac{15}{15.1}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the division to find the z-value:

ti84.instructions: Enter the division to find the z-value:

steps.title: Compare z-scores
steps.content:
Calculating the value gives:

$$z \approx 0.99$$

The Head of Department claims this student is **about 2 standard deviations** above average ($z=2$).

Comparing our result to the claim:

$$0.99 \neq 2$$

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
Since our calculated $z$-score is approximately 1, which is significantly different from the claimed value of 2, the claim is **not reasonable**.

In statistics, a $z$-score of 1 implies the score is 1 standard deviation above the mean, not 2.

steps.title: Establish the standard deviation
steps.content:
First, we determine the standard deviation $\sigma$ using the information provided. We are given two percentiles:
- $P(X > 72) = 0.2 \Rightarrow P(X \le 72) = 0.8$
- $P(X < 40) = 0.1$

Using the inverse normal function on a calculator, we find the corresponding $z$-scores:
$$z_{0.8} \approx 0.8416, \quad z_{0.1} \approx -1.2816$$

We set up standardized equations using the formula $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$:
$$72 - \mu = 0.8416\sigma, \quad 40 - \mu = -1.2816\sigma$$

Subtracting the second equation from the first to eliminate $\mu$:
$$32 = (0.8416 + 1.2816)\sigma = 2.1232\sigma \implies \sigma \approx 15.1$$

gdc.instructions: In Run-Matrix or Statistics, go to DIST > NORM > InvN. Enter Area: 0.8 and 0.1.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 3:invNorm. Enter area 0.8, then area 0.1.

steps.title: Calculate deviation for 2 sigma
steps.content:
The claim states that a score "15 points above the mean" corresponds to "2 standard deviations". Using the **Comparing Raw Value Deviations** approach, we calculate the actual raw score difference that corresponds to $2$ standard deviations ($2\sigma$).

Using $\sigma \approx 15.1$:
$$\text{Deviation for } 2\sigma = 2 \times 15.1 = 30.2 \text{ points}$$

steps.title: Compare values
steps.content:
Now we compare our calculated deviation with the deviation in the claim:
- **Claimed deviation:** $15$ points
- **Actual $2\sigma$ deviation:** $\approx 30.2$ points

We observe that $15$ is significantly different from $30.2$. In fact, 15 points is approximately $1$ standard deviation ($1\sigma$), not $2$.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content: Since the raw score difference for 2 standard deviations ($30.2$) is not close to the claimed difference ($15$), the Head of Department's claim is **not reasonable**.

parts.content: Next year the exam will be rescaled to $Y=aX+b$ so that $\operatorname{mean}(Y)=70$ and $\operatorname{sd}(Y)=12$. Find $a$ and $b$ (to 3 s.f.). Under this rescaling, what is the pass mark on the $Y$-scale corresponding to part 2?
parts.markscheme:
- **Calculate** the scaling factor $a$: $a = \frac{12}{\sigma} \approx \frac{12}{15.1} \approx 0.795$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Calculate** the constant $b$: $b = 70 - a\mu \approx 70 - 0.795(59.3) \approx 22.9$ <marks>A1</marks>
- **Calculate** the new pass mark: $c_Y = a(65.1) + b \approx 0.795(65.1) + 22.9 \approx 74.7$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Accept $c_Y \in [74.6, 74.8]$. Alternative method using $N(70, 12^2)$ yields $70 + 0.385(12) \approx 74.6$.</note>

steps.title: Identify given parameters
steps.content:
From the previous parts of the question, we have the original parameters for $X$ and the pass mark:

$$\mu_X \approx 59.3, \quad \sigma_X \approx 15.1, \quad \text{Pass mark}_X = 65.1$$

We are given the new target parameters for $Y$ in this part:

$$\mu_Y = 70, \quad \sigma_Y = 12$$

steps.title: Calculate scaling factor a
steps.content:
The standard deviation scales directly with the multiplicative factor $a$. We use the ratio of the new standard deviation to the old one:

$$a = \frac{\sigma_Y}{\sigma_X}$$

Substituting our values:

$$a \approx \frac{12}{15.1} \approx 0.795$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the division to find a

ti84.instructions: Enter the division to find a

steps.title: Calculate constant b
steps.content:
The mean is affected by both the scaling factor $a$ and the shift $b$. The relationship is $\mu_Y = a\mu_X + b$. Rearranging for $b$:

$$b = \mu_Y - a\mu_X$$

Substituting the values:

$$b \approx 70 - 0.795(59.3) \approx 22.9$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation for b

steps.title: Find new pass mark
steps.content:
Finally, we apply the linear transformation equation $Y = aX + b$ to the original pass mark of $65.1$ to find the new pass mark.

$$\text{Pass mark}_Y = a(65.1) + b \approx 0.795(65.1) + 22.9 \approx 74.7$$

So, the values are $a \approx 0.795$, $b \approx 22.9$, and the new pass mark is approximately $74.7$.

steps.title: Understand Z-score equivalence
steps.content:
The **Z-score Equivalence** method relies on the fact that a student's relative performance (their standardized score or z-score) remains unchanged under linear rescaling.

The z-score connects the value to the distribution parameters:

$$z = \frac{x - \mu_X}{\sigma_X} = \frac{y - \mu_Y}{\sigma_Y}$$

steps.title: Calculate Z-score of pass mark
steps.content:
First, we calculate the z-score associated with the original pass mark of $65.1$ using the original parameters $\mu_X = 59.3$ and $\sigma_X = 15.1$:

$$z = \frac{65.1 - 59.3}{15.1} \approx 0.3841$$

steps.title: Calculate new pass mark
steps.content:
Now, we apply this z-score to the new distribution parameters ($Y$) to find the new pass mark. We use the formula $y = \mu_Y + z\sigma_Y$ with $\mu_Y = 70$ and $\sigma_Y = 12$:

$$y = 70 + (0.3841)(12) = 70 + 4.609 \approx 74.6$$

Therefore, the new pass mark is approximately $74.6$.

parts.content: Find the values of $\mu$ and $\sigma$.
parts.markscheme:
- **Attempt** to set up the standardized equation for $P(X > 75) = 0.32$: $\frac{75 - \mu}{\sigma} = 0.468$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Attempt** to set up the standardized equation for $P(X \le 68) = 0.60$: $\frac{68 - \mu}{\sigma} = 0.253$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Solve** the system for $\sigma$: $\sigma = \frac{75 - 68}{0.468 - 0.253} \approx 32.6$ <marks>A1</marks>
- **Calculate** $\mu$: $\mu = 68 - 0.253(32.56) \approx 59.8$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Recall the standardization formula
steps.content:
To find the unknown mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, we need to convert the given $X$ values into standard normal $Z$-scores using the standardization formula:

$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

steps.title: Analyze the first probability
steps.content:
We are given that $P(X > 75) = 0.32$. The standard normal table (or inverse normal function) works with the area to the left (cumulative probability). 

We convert the "greater than" probability to "less than":
$$P(X \le 75) = 1 - P(X > 75) = 1 - 0.32 = 0.68$$

steps.title: Find the first z-score
steps.content:
Using a calculator, we find the $z$-score corresponding to an area of $0.68$ to the left.

$$z_1 = \text{invNorm}(0.68) \approx 0.468$$

gdc.instructions: Go to Statistics/Distribution mode, select Inverse Normal. Set Area = 0.68, μ = 0, σ = 1.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 3:invNorm. Enter area: 0.68, μ: 0, σ: 1.

calculator.description: Calculate the inverse normal for an area of 0.68

steps.title: Set up the first equation
steps.content:
Substitute $x = 75$ and $z = 0.468$ into the standardization formula:

$$0.468 = \frac{75 - \mu}{\sigma}$$ 

Rearranging this gives our first linear equation:
$$75 - \mu = 0.468\sigma \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu + 0.468\sigma = 75 \quad \text{(Equation 1)}$$

steps.title: Analyze the second condition
steps.content:
We are given that the **60th percentile is 68**. This means that $60\%$ of the data lies below $68$:

$$P(X \le 68) = 0.60$$

steps.title: Find the second z-score
steps.content:
Using the calculator, we find the $z$-score corresponding to an area of $0.60$.

$$z_2 = \text{invNorm}(0.60) \approx 0.253$$

gdc.instructions: Go to Statistics/Distribution mode, select Inverse Normal. Set Area = 0.60, μ = 0, σ = 1.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 3:invNorm. Enter area: 0.60, μ: 0, σ: 1.

calculator.description: Calculate the inverse normal for an area of 0.60

steps.title: Set up the second equation
steps.content:
Substitute $x = 68$ and $z = 0.253$ into the standardization formula:

$$0.253 = \frac{68 - \mu}{\sigma}$$ 

Rearranging gives our second linear equation:
$$68 - \mu = 0.253\sigma \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu + 0.253\sigma = 68 \quad \text{(Equation 2)}$$

steps.title: Solve the system for σ
steps.content:
We now have a system of two linear equations:
1. $\mu + 0.468\sigma = 75$
2. $\mu + 0.253\sigma = 68$

Subtract Equation 2 from Equation 1 to eliminate $\mu$:
$$(0.468\sigma - 0.253\sigma) = 75 - 68$$
$$0.215\sigma = 7$$

Solving for $\sigma$:
$$\sigma = \frac{7}{0.215} \approx 32.6$$

steps.title: Calculate μ
steps.content:
Substitute $\sigma \approx 32.558$ back into Equation 2:

$$\mu = 68 - 0.253(32.558)$$
$$\mu \approx 68 - 8.24$$
$$\mu \approx 59.8$$

calculator.description: Calculate mu using the unrounded value of sigma

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The values for the distribution are:

$$\mu \approx 59.8, \quad \sigma \approx 32.6$$

steps.title: Interpret probability conditions
steps.content:
First, we need to convert the given information into the standard form $P(X \le x)$ to use the normal distribution tables or calculator.

For the first condition, we are given the upper tail probability. We convert this to the area to the left:
$$P(X > 75) = 0.32 \Rightarrow P(X \le 75) = 1 - 0.32 = 0.68$$

For the second condition, the 60th percentile corresponds to the value below which 60% of the data falls:
$$P(X \le 68) = 0.60$$

steps.title: Find corresponding z-scores
steps.content:
Now we use the **inverse normal** function on a calculator to find the standardized $z$-scores that correspond to these cumulative probabilities.

For $P(Z \le z_1) = 0.68$:
$$z_1 \approx 0.468$$

For $P(Z \le z_2) = 0.60$:
$$z_2 \approx 0.253$$

steps.title: Apply Difference Ratio Method
steps.content:
The **Difference Ratio Method** calculates $\sigma$ directly by comparing the change in raw scores ($X$) to the change in $z$-scores ($Z$). Since $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$, the change in $X$ scales linearly with $Z$:

$$\Delta X = \sigma \cdot \Delta Z \Rightarrow \sigma = \frac{x_1 - x_2}{z_1 - z_2}$$

Substitute our values:
$$\sigma = \frac{75 - 68}{0.468 - 0.253}$$

steps.title: Calculate standard deviation
steps.content:
Now we compute the value of $\sigma$:

$$\sigma = \frac{7}{0.215} \approx 32.558$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures:
$$\sigma \approx 32.6$$

steps.title: Calculate the mean
steps.content:
Finally, we find $\mu$ by substituting $\sigma$ back into the standardized equation for one of our points, using the relation $x = \mu + z\sigma$ which rearranges to $\mu = x - z\sigma$.

Using the second point ($x=68$, $z=0.253$):
$$\begin{aligned} \mu &= 68 - (0.253)(32.558) \\ \mu &\approx 68 - 8.24 \\ \mu &\approx 59.8 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify distribution parameters
steps.content:
From the previous parts of the question, the probability of a student scoring 80 or more is $p = 0.267$. We are approximating a binomial distribution $W \sim \text{B}(120, 0.267)$.

First, calculate the mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$ for the normal approximation using the formulas from the data booklet.

steps.title: Calculate mean and SD
steps.content:
Substitute $n=120$ and $p=0.267$ into the formulas:

$$\mu = 120(0.267) = 32.04 \approx 32.0$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{120(0.267)(1-0.267)} = \sqrt{120(0.267)(0.733)} \approx 4.85$$

We will use these rounded values for the standardization step.

steps.title: Apply continuity correction
steps.content:
We need to estimate the probability that *at least* 25 students score 80 or more, which is $P(W \ge 25)$.

Since we are using a continuous normal distribution to approximate a discrete binomial distribution, we apply a **continuity correction**.

To include the discrete value 25 in our "at least" range, we start from the lower boundary of the interval representing 25. Therefore, we use a lower bound of **24.5**.

$$P(W \ge 25) \approx P(X_{\text{norm}} \ge 24.5)$$

steps.title: Standardize to Z-score
steps.content:
Now, standardize the value $x = 24.5$ to a Z-score using the mean $\mu = 32.0$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 4.85$.

$$Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

$$Z = \frac{24.5 - 32.0}{4.85}$$

$$Z \approx -1.546$$

The markscheme rounds this to $Z = -1.55$.

steps.content:

$$P(Z \ge -1.55) \approx 0.939$$

So, the estimated probability is **0.939**.

parts.content: The scores are rescaled using the transformation $Y = aX + b$. The new scores $Y$ have a mean of 70 and a standard deviation of 15. Find the values of $a$ and $b$, and the new distinction cutoff on the $Y$-scale.
parts.markscheme:
- **Calculate** scaling factor $a$: $a = \frac{15}{\sigma} \approx \frac{15}{32.56} \approx 0.461$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Calculate** shift term $b$: $b = 70 - a\mu \approx 70 - 0.461(59.8) \approx 42.4$ <marks>A1</marks>
- **Calculate** new cutoff $c_Y$: $c_Y = ac + b \approx 0.461(101.5) + 42.4 \approx 89.2$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify given parameters
steps.content:
First, we identify the parameters of the original distribution $X$ and the target distribution $Y$. Based on the provided specification and standard normal calculations (or results from previous parts of the question), the original parameters are approximately:

- Mean: $\mu_X \approx 59.8$
- Standard Deviation: $\sigma_X \approx 32.56$
- Distinction cutoff: $x \approx 101.5$ (typically the top 10%)

The target parameters for the rescaled scores $Y$ are given as:

- Mean: $\mu_Y = 70$
- Standard Deviation: $\sigma_Y = 15$

The transformation is linear: $$Y = aX + b$$

highlights.text: mean of 70 and a standard deviation of 15

steps.title: Use standard deviation property
steps.content:
For a linear transformation $Y = aX + b$, the standard deviation scales by the factor $a$ (since $a$ must be positive to maintain the order of scores):

$$\sigma_Y = a \sigma_X$$

Substituting the known values to solve for $a$:

$$15 = a(32.56)$$

$$a = \frac{15}{32.56} \approx 0.461$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the new standard deviation by the old standard deviation.

desmos.instructions: Divide the new standard deviation by the old standard deviation.

steps.title: Use expectation property
steps.content:
For the mean, the linear transformation applies directly:

$$\mathrm{E}(Y) = a\mathrm{E}(X) + b \implies \mu_Y = a\mu_X + b$$

Substitute $\mu_Y = 70$, $\mu_X = 59.8$, and our calculated $a \approx 0.461$:

$$70 = 0.461(59.8) + b$$

Solving for $b$:

$$70 = 27.5678 + b$$

$$b = 70 - 27.5678 \approx 42.4$$

gdc.instructions: Subtract the scaled mean from the target mean.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the scaled mean from the target mean.

steps.title: Calculate new distinction cutoff
steps.content:
Finally, we find the new distinction cutoff on the $Y$-scale by applying the same transformation to the original cutoff $x \approx 101.5$:

$$Y_{\text{cutoff}} = a(X_{\text{cutoff}}) + b$$

$$Y_{\text{cutoff}} = 0.461(101.5) + 42.4$$

$$Y_{\text{cutoff}} \approx 46.79 + 42.4 \approx 89.2$$

So, the values are $a \approx 0.461$, $b \approx 42.4$, and the new cutoff is $89.2$.

steps.title: Determine parameters for X
steps.content:
First, we determine the mean $\mu_X$ and standard deviation $\sigma_X$ of the original scores using the given probabilities.

From the question:
1. $P(X > 75) = 0.32 \Rightarrow P(X \le 75) = 0.68$. The corresponding z-score is $z_1 \approx 0.468$.
2. The 60th percentile is 68, so $P(X \le 68) = 0.60$. The corresponding z-score is $z_2 \approx 0.253$.

These give us two equations for $X$:
$$\begin{aligned} 75 &= \mu_X + 0.468\sigma_X \\ 68 &= \mu_X + 0.253\sigma_X \end{aligned}$$

Solving this system yields $\sigma_X \approx 32.56$ and $\mu_X \approx 59.8$.

calculator.description: Calculate sigma by subtracting the equations: 7 = (0.468 - 0.253) * sigma

steps.title: Form simultaneous equations
steps.content:
To find $a$ and $b$ for the transformation $Y = aX + b$, we map known points from the $X$ distribution to the $Y$ distribution. We use the mean and a point one standard deviation above the mean.

**Point 1 (Mean):**
The mean of $X$ ($59.8$) maps to the mean of $Y$ ($70$).
$$70 = a(59.8) + b \quad \text{(Equation 1)}$$

**Point 2 (Mean + 1 SD):**
A value one standard deviation above the mean in $X$ ($59.8 + 32.56 = 92.36$) maps to one standard deviation above the mean in $Y$ ($70 + 15 = 85$).
$$85 = a(92.36) + b \quad \text{(Equation 2)}$$

highlights.text: mean of 70 and a standard deviation of 15

steps.title: Solve for scaling factor a
steps.content:
Subtract Equation 1 from Equation 2 to eliminate $b$:

$$\begin{aligned} (85 - 70) &= a(92.36 - 59.8) \\ 15 &= a(32.56) \\ a &= \frac{15}{32.56} \\ a &\approx 0.461 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for shift term b
steps.content:
Substitute $a \approx 0.461$ back into Equation 1 to find $b$:

$$\begin{aligned} 70 &= 0.461(59.8) + b \\ 70 &= 27.5678 + b \\ b &= 70 - 27.5678 \\ b &\approx 42.4 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate b using the value of a

steps.title: Calculate new distinction cutoff
steps.content:
We need to find the new distinction cutoff on the $Y$-scale. Based on the markscheme, the distinction cutoff on the $X$-scale is $x = 101.5$ (this corresponds to the top 10%, i.e., $P(X > x) = 0.10$).

Apply the transformation formula using our calculated values for $a$ and $b$:

$$\begin{aligned} c_Y &= a(c_X) + b \\ c_Y &= 0.461(101.5) + 42.4 \\ c_Y &= 46.7915 + 42.4 \\ c_Y &\approx 89.2 \end{aligned}$$

parts.markscheme:
- Identify the z-scores corresponding to the given probabilities:

- Set up the standardized equations:
  $\frac{40-\mu}{\sigma} = -2.0$ and $\frac{50-\mu}{\sigma} = 1.0$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Form a system of linear equations:
  $40 - \mu = -2\sigma$
  $50 - \mu = \sigma$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Solve the system for $\mu$ and $\sigma$:

steps.title: Identify the method
steps.content:
To find the mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$ when given two probabilities for a normal distribution, we must use the **Standardization & Simultaneous Equations** method. This topic is covered in **SL 4.12** of the syllabus.

We will transform the given $X$ values into standard $z$-scores using the standardization formula:

$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

steps.title: Find z-scores using calculator
steps.content:
First, we use the inverse normal function on our calculator to find the $z$-scores that correspond to the given cumulative probabilities.

For $P(L < 40) = 0.0228$, we need the $z$-score where the area to the left is $0.0228$. 
For $P(L < 50) = 0.8413$, we need the $z$-score where the area to the left is $0.8413$.

steps.title: State the z-scores
steps.content:
From the calculator, we obtain the following $z$-scores:

For area $0.0228$:
$$z_1 = -2.0$$

For area $0.8413$:
$$z_2 = 1.0$$

These integers indicate the values are exactly 2 standard deviations below and 1 standard deviation above the mean.

steps.title: Set up simultaneous equations
steps.content:
Now we substitute our $x$ values ($40$ and $50$) and their corresponding $z$-scores ($-2$ and $1$) into the standardization formula $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ to create a system of two equations:

$$\begin{aligned} 1) && -2 &= \frac{40 - \mu}{\sigma} \\ 2) && 1 &= \frac{50 - \mu}{\sigma} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Form linear equations
steps.content:
Rearrange the equations to make them linear by multiplying both sides by $\sigma$:

$$\begin{aligned} 1) && -2\sigma &= 40 - \mu && \Rightarrow && \mu - 2\sigma = 40 \\ 2) && \sigma &= 50 - \mu && \Rightarrow && \mu + \sigma = 50 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for sigma
steps.content:
We can solve this system by elimination. Subtract the first equation from the second:

$$\begin{aligned} (\mu + \sigma) - (\mu - 2\sigma) &= 50 - 40 \\ 3\sigma &= 10 \\ \sigma &= \frac{10}{3} \approx 3.33 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 10 / 3

ti84.instructions: Calculate 10 / 3

steps.title: Solve for mu
steps.content:
Now substitute $\sigma = \frac{10}{3}$ back into the second equation to find $\mu$:

$$\begin{aligned} \mu &= 50 - \sigma \\ \mu &= 50 - \frac{10}{3} \\ \mu &= \frac{150}{3} - \frac{10}{3} \\ \mu &= \frac{140}{3} \approx 46.7 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 50 - (10/3)

ti84.instructions: Calculate 50 - (10/3)

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The mean and standard deviation are:

$$\mu = 46.7 \text{ cm} \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma = 3.33 \text{ cm}$$

We are given two conditions for the lengths of fish $L$, which follow a normal distribution $L \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

2. For length 50 cm: $$P(L < 50) = 0.8413$$

To find the unknown mean ($\mu$) and standard deviation ($\sigma$), we first need to convert these probabilities into standardized $z$-scores.

Using the inverse normal function on your calculator, determine the $z$-scores corresponding to the cumulative probabilities.

steps.title: Calculate standard deviation
steps.content:
Using the **Difference & Ratio Approach**, we find $\sigma$ by calculating the ratio of the difference in $x$-values to the difference in $z$-scores.

Calculate the differences:
$$\begin{aligned} \Delta x &= 50 - 40 = 10 \\ \Delta z &= 1.0 - (-2.0) = 3.0 \end{aligned}$$

Now, divide $\Delta x$ by $\Delta z$ to find $\sigma$:
$$\sigma = \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta z} = \frac{10}{3} \approx 3.33$$

steps.title: Calculate mean
steps.content:

Using $x_2 = 50$ and $z_2 = 1.0$:
$$\begin{aligned} \mu &= x_2 - z_2\sigma \\ \mu &= 50 - 1.0\left(\frac{10}{3}\right) \\ \mu &= 50 - 3.33\dots \\ \mu &= 46.66\dots \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: Find the probability that a caught fish is a trophy fish given that it is not released.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the probability of a fish being released ($L < 25$):
  $P(L<25) = P\left(Z < \frac{25-46.67}{3.33}\right) = P(Z < -6.5) \approx 0$
  So $P(\text{not released}) = P(L > 25) \approx 1$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate the probability of a trophy fish ($L > 55$):
  $P(L>55) = P\left(Z > \frac{55-46.67}{3.33}\right) = P(Z > 2.5) = 0.00621$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Apply the conditional probability formula:
  $P(L>55 \mid L>25) = \frac{P(L>55 \cap L>25)}{P(L>25)} = \frac{P(L>55)}{P(L>25)}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the final probability:
  $\frac{0.00621}{1} = 0.00621$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Define the events
steps.content:
First, let's identify the events based on the question conditions. We need to find the probability of a fish being a "trophy fish" given it is "not released".

Let $L$ be the length of the fish.
- **Trophy fish**: The question states these are fish "longer than 55 cm". So, we want the event $L > 55$.
- **Released fish**: The question states fish "shorter than 25 cm are released".
- **Not released**: This corresponds to the complement event, $L \ge 25$.

We need to find the conditional probability $$P(L > 55 \mid L \ge 25)$$.

highlights.text: longer than 55 cm are considered trophy fish
Fish shorter than 25 cm are released

steps.title: Apply conditional probability formula
steps.content:
Using the conditional probability formula from the data booklet:

$$P(A|B)=\frac{P(A\cap B)}{P(B)}$$

Substituting our events:
$$P(L > 55 \mid L \ge 25) = \frac{P(L > 55 \cap L \ge 25)}{P(L \ge 25)}$$

Notice that if a fish is longer than 55 cm ($L > 55$), it is automatically longer than 25 cm ($L \ge 25$). Therefore, the intersection of these events is simply the trophy condition:
$$P(L > 55 \cap L \ge 25) = P(L > 55)$$

Our formula simplifies to:
$$P(L > 55 \mid L \ge 25) = \frac{P(L > 55)}{P(L \ge 25)}$$

steps.title: Calculate P(Not Released)
steps.content:
To calculate the probabilities, we use the distribution parameters derived from the earlier information ($P(L<40)=0.0228$ and $P(L<50)=0.8413$). Solving that system yields a mean $$\mu \approx 46.67$$ and standard deviation $$\sigma \approx 3.33$$.

First, we find the probability of the denominator, $P(L \ge 25)$. We convert the boundary length 25 to a Z-score:

$$Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{25 - 46.67}{3.33}$$

$$Z \approx -6.5$$

The probability of a Z-score being greater than -6.5 is effectively 1 (since -6.5 is extremely far to the left on the normal curve).

$$P(L \ge 25) = P(Z > -6.5) \approx 1$$
