steps.title: Calculate P(Trophy)
steps.content:
Next, we find the numerator, $P(L > 55)$. Convert the boundary length 55 to a Z-score:

$$Z = \frac{55 - 46.67}{3.33}$$

$$Z \approx 2.5$$

Now we find the probability that $Z > 2.5$:
$$P(L > 55) = P(Z > 2.5)$$

Using a calculator or standard normal table:
$$P(Z > 2.5) \approx 0.00621$$

steps.title: Calculate final probability
steps.content:
Finally, we substitute these values back into our conditional probability formula:

$$\begin{aligned} P(L > 55 \mid L \ge 25) &= \frac{P(L > 55)}{P(L \ge 25)} \\ &= \frac{0.00621}{1} \\ &= 0.00621 \end{aligned}$$

The probability that a caught fish is a trophy fish given that it is not released is **0.00621**.

steps.title: Identify conditional probability
steps.content:
We need to find the probability that a fish is a trophy fish given that it is not released. We can express this using the conditional probability formula:

$$P(\text{Trophy} \mid \text{Not Released}) = \frac{P(\text{Trophy} \cap \text{Not Released})}{P(\text{Not Released})}$$

- **Trophy fish**: $L > 55$
- **Not released**: $L \ge 25$ (since fish shorter than 25 cm are released)

Because any fish longer than 55 cm is automatically longer than 25 cm, the intersection of these events is simply being a trophy fish ($L > 55$).

$$P(L > 55 \mid L \ge 25) = \frac{P(L > 55)}{P(L \ge 25)}$$

steps.title: Find distribution parameters
steps.content:
First, we need to determine the mean ($\mu$) and standard deviation ($\sigma$) from the probabilities given in the question specification. 

Using the inverse normal function on a calculator:
- $P(L < 40) = 0.0228$ corresponds to a z-score of $z \approx -2$
- $P(L < 50) = 0.8413$ corresponds to a z-score of $z \approx 1$

Set up simultaneous equations using the standardization formula $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$:
$$\begin{aligned} 1) && -2 &= \frac{40 - \mu}{\sigma} && \Rightarrow && \mu - 2\sigma = 40 \\ 2) && 1 &= \frac{50 - \mu}{\sigma} && \Rightarrow && \mu + \sigma = 50 \end{aligned}$$

Solving these equations (subtracting eq 1 from eq 2):
$$\begin{aligned} 3\sigma &= 10 && \Rightarrow && \sigma = \frac{10}{3} \approx 3.333 \\ \mu &= 50 - \frac{10}{3} && = && \frac{140}{3} \approx 46.667 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine mean and standard deviation
steps.content:
First, we determine the mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$ using the given probabilities. We find the $z$-scores corresponding to the cumulative probabilities:

$$\begin{aligned} P(L < 40) &= 0.0228 \Rightarrow z_1 \approx -2 \\ P(L < 50) &= 0.8413 \Rightarrow z_2 \approx 1 \end{aligned}$$

Setting up simultaneous equations using $x = \mu + z\sigma$:
$$\begin{aligned} 40 &= \mu - 2\sigma \\ 50 &= \mu + 1\sigma \end{aligned}$$

Solving this system gives $\sigma = \frac{10}{3}$ (approx $3.33$) and $\mu = \frac{140}{3}$ (approx $46.7$).

Trophy fish are defined as those longer than 55 cm. We need to find the probability of catching a trophy fish, $P(L > 55)$.

Using the **Standardization Approach**, we convert the length $x = 55$ to a standard score $z$:

$$z = \frac{55 - 46.67}{3.33} = 2.5$$

The $z$-score is exactly $2.5$.

steps.title: Find the tail probability
steps.content:
Now we find the probability of a fish being longer than 55 cm, which corresponds to the area to the right of $z = 2.5$ on the standard normal curve.

$$P(L > 55) = P(Z > 2.5)$$

Using standard normal tables or a calculator:
$$P(Z > 2.5) = 1 - P(Z < 2.5) \approx 1 - 0.99379 = 0.00621$$

The fisherman catches $n = 100$ fish. To find the expected number of trophy fish, we multiply the total number of fish by the probability of catching a trophy fish.

Using the formula for expectation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{E}(X) &= np \\ \text{E}(X) &= 100 \times 0.00621 = 0.621 \end{aligned}$$

The expected number of trophy fish is $0.621$.

steps.title: Define the standardization formula

To find the unknown mean ($\mu$) and standard deviation ($\sigma$), we use the standardization formula, which links the values from a general normal distribution $X$ to the standard normal distribution $Z$. The formula is:

$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

steps.title: Calculate Z-scores

Use the inverse normal function on your calculator to find the $z$-scores corresponding to the given probabilities. We are looking for the $z$-values where the area to the left is $0.15$ and $0.85$.

For $P(T < 20) = 0.15$:
$$z_1 = \Phi^{-1}(0.15) \approx -1.0364$$

For $P(T < 30) = 0.85$:
$$z_2 = \Phi^{-1}(0.85) \approx 1.0364$$

**Important:** Keep the precise value ($1.03643...$) in your calculator. Rounding to $1.036$ too early will lead to an incorrect final answer.

steps.title: Set up the system of equations
steps.content:
Substitute the known $x$ values ($20$ and $30$) and their corresponding $z$-scores into the standardization formula to create a system of two simultaneous linear equations.

From $x = 20$:
$$\frac{20 - \mu}{\sigma} = -1.0364 \implies 20 - \mu = -1.0364\sigma \quad (1)$$

From $x = 30$:
$$\frac{30 - \mu}{\sigma} = 1.0364 \implies 30 - \mu = 1.0364\sigma \quad (2)$$

steps.title: Solve for the mean
steps.content:
Add equation (1) and equation (2) together to eliminate $\sigma$. Because the Z-scores are symmetric ($\pm 1.0364$), the $\sigma$ terms cancel out perfectly.

$$\begin{aligned} (20 - \mu) + (30 - \mu) &= -1.0364\sigma + 1.0364\sigma \\ 50 - 2\mu &= 0 \\ 2\mu &= 50 \\ \mu &= 25 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for standard deviation
steps.content:
Substitute $\mu = 25$ back into equation (2) to solve for $\sigma$. Use the precise value of $z$ for accuracy.

$$\begin{aligned} 30 - 25 &= 1.0364\sigma \\ 5 &= 1.0364\sigma \\ \sigma &= \frac{5}{1.0364...} \\ \sigma &\approx 4.8242 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State final answers
steps.content:
Finally, round the standard deviation to 3 significant figures as required.

$$\mu = 25, \quad \sigma = 4.82$$

(Note: If you rounded $z$ to $1.036$ earlier, you would get $\sigma = 4.83$, which is incorrect.)

steps.title: Recognize symmetry in probabilities
steps.content:
First, let's look at the given probabilities. We are told that $P(T < 20) = 0.15$ and $P(T < 30) = 0.85$.

Notice that the sum of these probabilities is $0.15 + 0.85 = 1$. In a normal distribution, the bell curve is symmetric. Since the area to the left of $T = 30$ is $0.85$, the area to the right is $1 - 0.85 = 0.15$.

Because the tail area below $20$ ($0.15$) is exactly equal to the tail area above $30$ ($0.15$), the values $20$ and $30$ must be equidistant from the mean $\mu$.

steps.title: Calculate the mean
steps.content:
Since $20$ and $30$ are symmetric about the mean, the mean $\mu$ is simply the midpoint of these two values.

$$\mu = \frac{20 + 30}{2}$$

$$\mu = 25$$

So, the mean time is $25$ minutes.

steps.title: Analyze the symmetry
steps.content:
First, let's look at the given probabilities to find the center of the distribution using the **Symmetry Deduction** approach.

We are given:
$$P(T < 20) = 0.15$$
$$P(T < 30) = 0.85$$

The normal distribution is symmetric. Notice that the probability above 30 is the complement of the probability below 30:
$$P(T > 30) = 1 - P(T < 30)$$
$$P(T > 30) = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15$$

Since the area to the left of 20 ($0.15$) is identical to the area to the right of 30 ($0.15$), the values 20 and 30 must be equidistant from the mean.

steps.title: Calculate the mean
steps.content:
Because of this symmetry, the mean $\mu$ lies exactly in the middle of $20$ and $30$.

We calculate the midpoint:
$$\mu = \frac{20 + 30}{2} = 25$$

So, the normal curve is centered at $t = 25$.

steps.title: Find z-scores
steps.content:
First, we use the inverse normal function on your calculator to find the z-scores associated with the given cumulative probabilities.

For $P(T < 20) = 0.15$:
$$z_1 = \Phi^{-1}(0.15) \approx -1.036$$

For $P(T < 30) = 0.85$:
$$z_2 = \Phi^{-1}(0.85) \approx 1.036$$

Notice that because $0.15$ and $0.85$ sum to $1$, the z-scores are symmetric (equal in magnitude, opposite in sign).

steps.title: Set up simultaneous equations
steps.content:
Using the standardization formula from the data booklet, we set up two equations linking the x-values ($20$ and $30$) to their z-scores.

$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Substituting our values:
1) $$-1.036 = \frac{20 - \mu}{\sigma}$$
2) $$1.036 = \frac{30 - \mu}{\sigma}$$

steps.title: Solve for the mean
steps.content:
We solve the simultaneous equations for the mean $\mu$. By symmetry, since the z-scores are opposites ($z_1 = -z_2$), the mean must be exactly in the middle of the x-values.

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{20 - \mu}{\sigma} &= -\frac{30 - \mu}{\sigma} \\ 20 - \mu &= -(30 - \mu) \\ 20 - \mu &= -30 + \mu \\ 50 &= 2\mu \\ \mu &= 25 \end{aligned}$$

The distribution is centred at $\mu = 25$.
